title: Why Sort Gaussians into a 2D Grid?
content:
## The Core Insight

3D Gaussian Splatting is **order-invariant**: a set of Gaussians represents the same scene regardless of the order they appear in the data array. This means there are \(N!\) equivalent representations for a scene with \(N\) Gaussians. Among all these permutations, most are incompressible — but some are highly compressible.

The key question is: **can we find the permutation that makes the data most compressible?**

## The Image Compression Analogy

Consider an extreme version of this problem with image pixels. A 500×500 pixel image has 250,000 pixels. If you randomly shuffle all pixels, you destroy all spatial correlation — a lossless codec like PNG must essentially store every pixel value independently.

Experimental results (from Barthel et al., FLAS paper) on a natural image:

| Arrangement | PNG size | Reduction vs. shuffled |
|---|---|---|
| Shuffled pixels | 626 KB | 1.0× (baseline) |
| Original image | 496 KB | 1.26× |
| Sorted (by color) | 199 KB | 3.15× |

For JPEG (lossy):

| Arrangement | JPEG size | Reduction vs. shuffled |
|---|---|---|
| Shuffled | 298 KB | 1.0× |
| Original | 174 KB | 1.71× |
| Sorted | 33 KB | **9.03×** |

Sorting pixels by value creates a smooth color gradient — adjacent pixels become similar, maximizing local correlation. Codecs exploit local correlation via prediction and transform coding (DCT in JPEG). **Sorted data is up to 9× more compressible under a lossy codec.**

## Attribute-to-Grid Reshaping

The same principle applies to 3DGS. A single Gaussian has 59 attributes. A scene with \(N\) Gaussians can be viewed as \(N \times 59\) matrix. This matrix can be **reshaped into a 2D grid** (e.g., \(\sqrt{N} \times \sqrt{N} \times 59\)) and then treated as a multi-channel image.

For \(N = 65536\) Gaussians: a \(256 \times 256 \times 59\) array of values, which can be split into 59 grayscale images (or grouped into multi-channel images), each of size \(256 \times 256\).

If these 59 images are smooth (nearby grid cells have similar values), standard image codecs (JPEG, JPEG XL, WebP, HEIC) will compress them effectively — with no 3DGS-specific codec needed.

## The Sorting Problem

The hard part: how do we **assign Gaussians to grid positions** such that the resulting attribute images are smooth?

This is equivalent to finding a permutation \(\pi\) of the \(N\) Gaussians that, when they are placed into the \(\sqrt{N} \times \sqrt{N}\) grid in order \(\pi\), minimizes the differences between adjacent grid cells.

For \(N = 65536\) Gaussians, the number of possible permutations is \(65536!\) — an astronomically large search space. Brute force is completely infeasible. We need efficient algorithms that find near-optimal orderings in polynomial time.

## Why Position Is the Natural Sorting Key

Gaussian attributes are not independent: nearby Gaussians in 3D space tend to have similar colors, similar scales, and similar orientations (they all lie on the same surface). By sorting Gaussians primarily by their 3D positions, we naturally cluster attributionally similar Gaussians together in the grid.

The SOG paper (Morgenstern et al., ECCV 2024) uses a weighted combination of all attributes as the sorting key:

\[c = \sum_i \omega_i A_i\]

where \(\omega_i\) are per-attribute weights (position weighted most heavily, since it correlates most strongly with the other attributes).
